title: SEGMENT 13 — Cell therapy: autologous dreams and allogeneic banks

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Cell therapy: autologous dreams and allogeneic banks
Now we get to the part of the iPSC story that originally generated the most excitement, and that has, in some ways, been the slowest to deliver: cell therapy.
Using iPSC-derived cells as transplanted tissue, to replace what a patient has lost.
The original dream was autologous therapy.
You take a skin biopsy from a patient with Parkinson's disease.
You reprogram the fibroblasts to iPSCs.
You differentiate them to dopamine neurons.
You inject those neurons into the patient's substantia nigra, where dopamine neurons are dying.
The transplanted cells take up residence, integrate into the existing circuitry, and produce dopamine.
The patient improves.
And because the cells are genetically identical to the patient — they came from the patient's own skin — there is no immune rejection.
The immune system sees self, not foreign.
No need for lifelong immunosuppression.
The analogy is: make your own replacement parts — your skin cell becomes a beta cell goes back into your pancreas, no immune rejection.
That's the dream.
The reality has bumped into several practical problems.
First, cost and time.
Making patient-specific iPSCs takes months and costs tens of thousands of dollars per patient.
Differentiating those iPSCs to clinical-grade cells takes more months.
By the time you have the cells ready, the patient may be much sicker.
For a slow-progressing disease this might be acceptable; for an aggressive one, it's a real problem.
Second, manufacturing complexity.
Every patient's iPSCs are slightly different.
Differentiation protocols that work beautifully for one line might be less efficient for another.
Quality control has to be done individually for each batch.
It's the opposite of mass manufacturing — every dose is bespoke.
Third, regulatory burden.
The FDA, sensibly, wants every batch of cells used in a patient to meet the same safety and identity standards.
Doing that QC for individual patient batches is staggeringly expensive.
For these reasons, the field has increasingly pivoted toward allogeneic therapy — using iPSCs derived from a small set of universal donors, not from each individual patient.
You generate a library of well-characterized iPSC lines, each from a different donor with a specific HLA type — the genes that determine which tissue types your immune system recognizes as self.
If the bank is large enough, most patients can be matched to a line with a compatible HLA type, the way blood banks match donors to recipients.
The cells aren't a perfect match to the patient, but they're close enough that immune rejection is manageable, often with mild immunosuppression rather than lifelong heavy doses.

Some efforts go even further — engineering "universal" iPSC lines where the HLA genes have been edited out entirely, so the cells are invisible to the immune system and can be transplanted into anyone.
There's a tradeoff here, and it's worth being clear about it.
Autologous iPSCs avoid rejection entirely but cost a fortune per patient.
Allogeneic iPSCs are mass-producible but require some immune management.
The field's bet, increasingly, is that allogeneic therapies will be the route to widespread clinical use — because medicine cannot afford to do individual reprogramming for every patient.
A few companies, particularly in Japan where Yamanaka's CiRA institute has built a national iPSC bank, are betting hard on this approach.
Off-the-shelf iPSC-derived therapies, available like blood products, matched approximately to the patient's HLA type, is the operational vision.
Whether that vision pans out — whether the immune management really stays manageable, whether the cost economics work — is being tested right now, in dozens of ongoing clinical trials.
Let's look at a few of them.
